axioms.name: Mimesis (Imitation)
axioms.statement: All art is imitation (mimesis)—poetry imitates action, character, and thought.

axioms.name: Natural Instinct
axioms.statement: Imitation is natural to humans; we learn through imitation and take pleasure in recognizing representations.

axioms.name: Universal Truth
axioms.statement: Poetry is more philosophical than history—it expresses the universal, not merely the particular.

axioms.name: Probable Impossible
axioms.statement: The probable impossible is preferable to the improbable possible.

axioms.name: Necessity and Probability
axioms.statement: Everything must follow from necessity or probability; nothing should happen by chance.

axioms.name: Correctness Standard
axioms.statement: The standard of correctness differs between poetry and other arts.

levels.name: TRAGEDY
levels.description: Imitation of an action that is serious, complete, and of magnitude

levels.name: WHOLE
levels.description: Complete unit with Beginning, Middle, and End

core_algorithms.name: The Unity Principle
core_algorithms.purpose: Verify the organic necessity of every part of the plot
core_algorithms.pseudocode: FOR each_part IN whole: IF whole.disturbed_by_removal(each_part): part = ORGANIC; ELSE: part = INORGANIC; REMOVE inorganic_parts();

core_algorithms.name: Tragic Effect Algorithm
core_algorithms.purpose: Generate catharsis through the proper configuration of pity and fear
core_algorithms.pseudocode: pity = unmerited_misfortune(character); fear = misfortune_of_similar_to_us(character); IF plot.triggers(pity) AND plot.triggers(fear): RETURN CATHARSIS; ELSE: RETURN FAILURE;

core_algorithms.name: Simple vs. Complex Plots
core_algorithms.purpose: Construct a plot using structural reversal and recognition
core_algorithms.pseudocode: 1. Change fortune (good to bad); 2. Action veers to opposite (REVERSAL); 3. Ignorance becomes knowledge (RECOGNITION); 4. Ensure 2 and 3 arise from internal structure;

core_algorithms.name: Hamartia Principle
core_algorithms.purpose: Configure the protagonist's fall through error rather than vice
core_algorithms.pseudocode: protagonist = renowned + prosperous + NOT_eminently_good + NOT_utter_villain; fall_cause = hamartia (error/frailty); IF fall_cause == vice: RETURN FAILURE; RETURN tragic_protagonist;

core_algorithms.name: The Relationship Proximity Rule
core_algorithms.purpose: Maximize pity by placing suffering between intimates
core_algorithms.pseudocode: IF killer AND victim ARE near/dear: pity = MAXIMUM; ELIF killer AND victim ARE strangers: pity = NONE; ELSE: pity = MINIMAL; RETURN pity_score;

diagnostic_questions.question: Does every event follow by necessity or probability?
If a part is removed, is the whole disturbed?
Does the character's action follow from their nature?
Does the unraveling arise from the plot itself (not a machine)?
Is the fall caused by an error (hamartia) rather than vice?
Does the reversal produce the opposite of the intended effect?
Is the recognition integrated with the incidents of the plot?
Is the probable impossible preferred over the improbable possible?
Are characters good, proper, true to life, and consistent?
Does the suffering occur between near or dear ones?

core_operations: PLOT > CHARACTER > THOUGHT > DICTION > SONG > SPECTACLE
Propter hoc (because of) > Post hoc (after this)
key_constraints: No Deus Ex Machina within the action
Protagonist falls by hamartia (error), not vice
Unified action centers on ONE action, not one hero
Magnitude must be perspicuous (graspable by memory)
